frontmatter.title: 11. Ethics and politics of Creative AI
frontmatter.subtitle: Copyright, bias, labour, sustainability, and consent

children.value: It would be easy to spend an entire course on the wonders of Creative AI without ever asking the harder question:
This chapter is the place where we ask it directly, with examples grounded in the tools you have been using all semester.

The aim is not to make you cynical or paralysed. It is to give you the vocabulary, the cases, and a small toolkit of personal decisions, so that when an AI tool lands on your work or in your inbox, you can think
about it instead of in slogans.
A simple frame: harm, benefit, and to whom

children.label: A simple frame: harm, benefit, and to whom

children.value: For every Creative AI system, four questions get you most of the way to a useful ethical position:
(Users? The company? Specific groups? Society at large?)
Who is harmed, or could be?
(Workers in the training data? Users? Bystanders? The planet?)
You will see versions of these four questions in every serious AI ethics framework, from the
European Parliament,Council of the European Union, 2024
Recommendation on the Ethics of AI
Now we apply them across five concrete topics.
Topic 1 — Copyright, consent, and training data

children.label: Topic 1 — Copyright, consent, and training data

children.value: The biggest open legal question in 2026 is
whether training a model on copyrighted material is fair use
. Different jurisdictions have given different answers, and many cases are still being litigated.
Most foundation models were trained on
that include copyrighted text, images, music, and code, without explicit permission from the authors.
fair use / fair dealing / “text and data mining” exception
Authors, artists, photographers, musicians, and game studios argue it is
The legal landscape: in the EU, there is a
) will set the doctrine for the next decade.
If you train or fine-tune, respect
for commercial or sensitive work where possible. Adobe Firefly, Getty AI, and a growing number of music-AI startups train on licensed corpora.

Should training data be opt-in (you must agree before your work is used) or opt-out (your work is used unless you object)? What changes for individual artists vs. institutions like libraries and universities?

Models inherit the distribution of their training data. If the data over-represents English, Western, urban, web-published, well-photographed, predominantly male material, the model will reflect that — sometimes obviously, sometimes invisibly.
Ask an image model for “a CEO” without further qualifiers. Count the proportion of men.
Ask a chat model for “a typical Norwegian breakfast”. How often does it list things actually eaten in Norway?
Ask a code model to write a sorting function. In which programming language does it default?
Ask a music model for “a wedding song”. From which culture?
biases. There are also subtler ones: stereotyped associations, missing dialects, accents that get mis-transcribed, faces that are not detected, languages that produce strictly worse output.
is the canonical critical text on this. Crawford
situates the question within wider structural inequalities.
for representation, especially in work that will be public.
Use refined prompts and reference images
when you see them. Many companies fix flagged biases in the next training cycle.
Choose tools that report bias evaluations
Generative AI sits on a foundation of human labour that is rarely visible:

— millions of workers, often in low-wage countries, label images, rank model outputs, and write fine-tuning examples. They make the model behave; they also bear the psychological cost of moderating violent and abusive material
Artists whose work was used for training
— often without consent, payment, or attribution.
asked to record samples that are later cloned to do work they would have been paid to do.
whose markets are reshaped by tools trained on their previous work.
This is not unique to AI — every wave of automation reorganises labour. But the speed and the source of the training data make it sharper than past waves.
that pay their labellers fairly and license their data.
to adopt AI policies that protect contractors and workers.
Many platforms now insert clauses about training on user content.
a lot of electricity, water, and rare materials
. Inference (everyday use) uses less per query but vastly more in aggregate. Strubell et al.
put this on the map in 2019 with their NLP-focused estimates; more recent estimates have widened the scope to include water for data-centre cooling and the embodied carbon of GPUs.
Major data centres in the Nordics, including some in Norway, are being built specifically for AI workloads. This is partly
our cheap clean electricity — a mixed blessing.
A single image generation can use roughly the energy of a smartphone charge for inference; training a frontier model uses on the order of a small town’s electricity for a year.
Reasoning-mode “thinking” models multiply inference compute by 10×–100× per query.
for routine tasks. Most jobs do not need the frontier.
Iterate locally before paying for the big cloud run.
Choose tools that publish their compute usage
Ask, in policy debates, whether the marginal benefit is worth the marginal energy.
Topic 5 — Authorship, authenticity, and the public sphere

children.label: Topic 5 — Authorship, authenticity, and the public sphere

children.value: Generative AI strains the basic categories of cultural life:
If a song is written 70% by Suno and 30% by you, who is the author? Spotify, the courts, and your conscience may give different answers.
A photograph of “the prime minister at a demonstration” is no longer evidence. Audio of “a friend asking for money” is no longer evidence. The default assumption of
European Parliament,Council of the European Union, 2024

Social platforms in 2026 are full of AI-generated content competing for human attention. Some of it is benign; some of it floods elections and public debate with low-quality, plausible-sounding noise.
Submitting AI-written essays as your own is academic dishonesty — but a generation of students has grown up with these tools, and the policies are still catching up.
When you publish AI-assisted work, say so. C2PA-style metadata is emerging as a standard.
When you receive a striking video, audio, or quote, check the provenance before sharing.
AI is not creating the apocalypse, and it is not just a fancy autocomplete.
Three habits worth committing to as a creator in 2026:
Every project: which tools, which prompts, which edits, which versions you kept and why. This protects you legally and is honest.
Treat a model like a freelancer.
Ask for credentials, check the work, give credit, do not assume good faith on copyright.
It is fine — and increasingly important — to say
“I am not using AI for this part.”
Pick a Creative AI tool you have used this semester. Spend the practice session writing a short
of it, using the four questions from the top of this chapter. Cover:
of training data (what the company says publicly).
— try a small probing set (e.g., 10 prompts that touch gender, geography, language).
— what do you know about the labellers and moderators?
— does the tool offer C2PA or watermarking?
Aim for 1000 words. This will be one of your most useful weekly logs.
Two teams of 3–4 students each. Each team randomly draws a position:
“Training generative models on copyrighted material is acceptable as fair use.”
“Training generative models on copyrighted material is not acceptable without per-rights-holder consent.”

You will defend the position you drew, regardless of your prior view. (This is a deliberate exercise — being able to make the strongest case for a view you disagree with is the most useful skill in ethics.)
Pick a Creative AI use you find uncomfortable. Write 300 words on why. Then write 200 words steelmanning the other side.
European Parliament,Council of the European Union, 2024
requires labelling of AI-generated content “interacting with humans”. How would you implement that for your own work?
Your future job will be done partly with AI. What conditions would have to hold for you to feel that this is good for you and good for others?
On the Dangers of Stochastic Parrots
Recommendation on the Ethics of AI
European Parliament,Council of the European Union, 2024
